text:
INTEGRATED PSMA PET/CT AND CONTRAST-ENHANCED CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with known metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, BRCA2 germline and somatic biallelic loss) undergoing restaging following 4 cycles of carboplatin/docetaxel combination chemotherapy. Prior PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 3 months ago demonstrated strongly PSMA-avid hepatic, pulmonary, osseous, and pelvic nodal metastases. Current PSA 19.8 ng/mL with rising trend after initial chemotherapy response. Referral from medical oncology for comprehensive restaging to guide next-line therapy planning. Testosterone level confirmed castrate.

TECHNIQUE:
Gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT: Following intravenous administration of 5.4 mCi (200 MBq) of Ga-68 labeled PSMA-11, whole-body PET acquisition was performed from skull vertex to mid-thigh 60 minutes post-injection in 3D mode, with attenuation-corrected and non-attenuation-corrected images reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Fused PET/CT images were reviewed in all planes.

Contrast-enhanced CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Helical CT acquisition was performed from the lung apices through the pubic symphysis following intravenous administration of Omnipaque 350 (100 mL) at 3 mL/sec, with portal venous phase (70-second delay) images of the abdomen and pelvis and pulmonary parenchymal window review of the chest. Oral contrast was administered.

Radiation dose: CT DLP 820 mGy-cm. Total effective dose estimated at approximately 12 mSv (combined CT and radiopharmaceutical contribution).

COMPARISON:
PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 3 months prior (at time of olaparib progression, prior to initiation of carboplatin/docetaxel). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 6 weeks prior (mid-chemotherapy assessment after 2 cycles), reviewed for correlation.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No intracranial PSMA-avid lesions. Physiologic PSMA uptake in the salivary glands is preserved bilaterally (parotid and submandibular glands), with symmetric distribution and no evidence of xerostomia-related glandular changes. No suspicious cervical lymphadenopathy. No new lesions in the skull base. Mild physiologic uptake in the lacrimal glands.

CHEST:
Lungs: A left lower lobe pulmonary nodule in the posterior basal segment measures 0.9 cm on the current CT, essentially unchanged compared to the prior study 6 weeks ago and to the PSMA PET/CT 3 months ago. This nodule demonstrates low-grade PSMA avidity with an SUVmax of 4.1, unchanged from the prior PSMA PET (SUVmax 6.2 on the prior study). No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. Lung parenchyma otherwise clear, without consolidation or ground-glass opacity. No hilar or mediastinal PSMA-avid lesions.

Mediastinum: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size or PSMA uptake. Cardiac silhouette is normal. No pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels are patent without aneurysm.

Bone (thoracic): New focal PSMA-avid lesion at the T5 vertebral body, demonstrating heterogeneous sclerosis on CT with a lytic component measuring approximately 1.5 cm, SUVmax 16.8. This is new compared to the prior PSMA PET/CT 3 months ago and was not seen on the 2-cycle restaging CT 6 weeks prior. Additional new PSMA-avid lesion at the T8 vertebral body, also with mixed lytic-sclerotic morphology on CT (approximately 1.2 cm), SUVmax 14.3, new from prior studies. Posterior left rib 6 lesion noted on recent CT appears more conspicuous on current imaging with associated cortical expansion and SUVmax 11.7; this was faintly noted on prior imaging and is now clearly progressive. No cord compression is identified, though the T5 and T8 lesions warrant close monitoring given their posterior element extension.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The previously described segment VII hepatic lesion in the posterior right lobe has markedly increased in size, now measuring 2.1 cm in greatest dimension compared to 0.8 cm on the CT performed approximately 6 weeks ago (after 2 cycles chemotherapy) and 1.4 cm at the time of olaparib progression. This lesion demonstrates intense PSMA uptake with an SUVmax of 12.4. Two new hepatic lesions are identified:
  - Segment II lesion (left lobe, lateral superior subsegment): 1.0 cm hypodense focus on CT, PSMA-avid with SUVmax 9.8. New from all prior studies.
  - Segment V lesion (right lobe, anterior inferior subsegment): 0.7 cm hypodense focus on CT, PSMA-avid with SUVmax 7.2. New from all prior studies.
The liver parenchyma is otherwise homogeneous without evidence of cirrhosis or portal hypertension. No biliary ductal dilatation. The hepatic veins and portal vein are patent.

Spleen: Normal in size at approximately 11 cm. No focal splenic lesions. No abnormal PSMA uptake.

Pancreas: Unremarkable without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or abnormal PSMA uptake.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology. No focal hypodense lesions or PSMA-avid foci.

Kidneys: Normal bilateral renal parenchyma without hydronephrosis. Note is made of physiologic PSMA excretion through the urinary collecting system. Right kidney measures 10.8 cm; left kidney 11.2 cm. No renal masses identified.

Retroperitoneum/Lymph nodes: No new retroperitoneal or paraaortic PSMA-avid lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS:
Lymph nodes: Progressive lymphadenopathy compared to prior studies. Right external iliac lymph node cluster now measures up to 1.7 cm (compared to 1.6 cm at olaparib progression and 1.0 cm on 2-cycle chemotherapy restaging CT), with SUVmax of 9.3 on PSMA PET. A new right obturator lymph node measures 0.8 cm with SUVmax 6.7, not seen on prior studies. Left external iliac chain demonstrates a new 0.6 cm lymph node with mild PSMA avidity (SUVmax 4.8), representing new contralateral pelvic nodal spread.

Prostate/Bladder: The prostate bed demonstrates physiologic or near-background PSMA activity, no discrete PSMA-avid mass identified in the prostate fossa (consistent with prior treatment/ADT effect). Bladder wall unremarkable.

Bone (pelvis and lower extremity):
  - Sacrum: New PSMA-avid lesion in the left sacral ala/S2 region, SUVmax 18.3, associated with mixed lytic-sclerotic morphology on CT (approximately 1.8 cm). New from prior studies. The previously known right sacral ala lesion remains present and appears stable in CT morphology, SUVmax 15.1.
  - Pubic rami: Bilateral new lesions in the pubic rami. Right superior pubic ramus: 1.1 cm sclerotic lesion with PSMA avidity, SUVmax 12.9 (new). Left inferior pubic ramus: 0.8 cm lesion, SUVmax 10.4 (new).
  - Right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region: Previously identified lesion with PSMA uptake now demonstrates cortical breakthrough on CT with soft tissue component approximately 0.4 cm in depth, SUVmax 21.2 (increased from SUVmax 18.1 at prior PSMA PET). This finding raises concern for impending pathologic fracture risk and is flagged for orthopedic surgery consideration.

SPINE (LUMBAR):
Known L3 and L4 vertebral body lesions: Both remain PSMA-avid; L3 SUVmax 19.6 (increased from 18.4 at prior PSMA PET), L4 SUVmax 16.2. Both lesions demonstrate predominantly sclerotic morphology on CT, consistent with treated disease with superimposed progression. No evidence of epidural extension or cord/cauda equina compromise at this level.

C7 VERTEBRA:
New PSMA-avid lesion at C7 vertebral body, demonstrating a small lytic focus (approximately 8 mm) with SUVmax 13.5. New from all prior studies. No prevertebral soft tissue mass. No cord compression. This finding is new and represents the most superior osseous lesion on this examination.

BONE LESION SUMMARY TABLE (selected):
- C7 vertebral body: NEW, SUVmax 13.5
- T5 vertebral body: NEW, SUVmax 16.8
- T8 vertebral body: NEW, SUVmax 14.3
- L3 vertebral body: Known, progressive (SUVmax 19.6)
- L4 vertebral body: Known, stable-to-progressive (SUVmax 16.2)
- Left posterior rib 6: Progressive, SUVmax 11.7
- Right iliac crest: Known, stable (SUVmax 14.0)
- Right sacral ala: Known, stable (SUVmax 15.1)
- Left sacral ala/S2: NEW, SUVmax 18.3
- Right femoral neck/intertrochanteric: Known, progressive with cortical breakthrough (SUVmax 21.2)
- Right superior pubic ramus: NEW, SUVmax 12.9
- Left inferior pubic ramus: NEW, SUVmax 10.4

Total number of discrete osseous lesions: at least 12 (including prior known + newly identified). Overall burden of osseous disease is substantially greater than at the time of the prior PSMA PET/CT performed 3 months ago.

PSMA UPTAKE ASSESSMENT:
All identified metastatic lesions demonstrate robust PSMA avidity. The ratio of tumor SUVmax to liver background SUVmax is markedly elevated, consistent with high PSMA expression. There are no identified metastatic lesions that are PSMA-negative (i.e., no discordant CT-positive/PET-negative disease). This pattern is favorable for Lu-177-PSMA-617 targeting if systemic therapy with this agent is considered. Physiologic PSMA uptake is present in kidneys, lacrimal glands, salivary glands, and liver (background), all within expected range.

IMPRESSION:
1. Progressive metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma with widespread disease progression compared to prior PSMA PET/CT obtained approximately 3 months ago and CT performed 6 weeks ago after 2 cycles of carboplatin/docetaxel chemotherapy.

2. Hepatic metastases: Clear progression, with the known right lobe segment VII lesion now 2.1 cm (previously 0.8 cm on 2-cycle CT, 1.4 cm at olaparib progression baseline), plus two new hepatic lesions in segments II and V -- representing worsening visceral disease burden.

3. Pulmonary: Left lower lobe pulmonary nodule stable at 0.9 cm with mildly decreased PSMA avidity compared to prior; no new pulmonary nodules.

4. Osseous disease: Substantial interval progression with multiple new lesions throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton including new involvement at C7, T5, T8, bilateral sacrum (new left sacral lesion), bilateral pubic rami, and progression of known lesions at L3, left rib 6, and right femoral neck. The right femoral neck/intertrochanteric lesion now demonstrates cortical breakthrough with a small soft tissue component, raising concern for impending pathologic fracture -- clinical/orthopedic evaluation recommended.

5. Nodal disease: Progressive bilateral pelvic nodal involvement with enlargement of right external iliac nodes, new right obturator node, and new left external iliac node.

6. All identified metastatic lesions are strongly PSMA-avid without discordant PSMA-negative sites, consistent with PSMA-expressing tumor biology favorable for targeted radioligand therapy.

7. No new intracranial metastases. No pleural effusions.

Overall, findings indicate widespread, strongly PSMA-avid progressive metastatic CRPC following 4 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy, with visceral (hepatic), osseous, and nodal progression. Impending fracture risk at the right femoral neck warrants urgent orthopedic evaluation.

Signed electronically by:
Attending Radiologist / Nuclear Medicine Physician
Department of Radiology and Nuclear Medicine
